/></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Font style tag</h2><p>The table below lists various Font Style Elements:</p><table class="wrapped" style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Tag <br />name</th><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Description</th></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>TT</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Renders teletyped or <br />monospaced text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>I</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Renders italic text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>B</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Renders bold text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>BIG</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders text in large <br />font.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>SMALL</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders text in small <br />font.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>STRIKE and S</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders strikethrough <br />text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>U</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders underlined <br />text.</td></tr></tbody></table><ul><li>TT <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</li><li>I <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li><li>B <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li><li>BIG <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font size=”5”&gt;</li><li>SMALL <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font size=”1”&gt;</li><li>STRIKE and S <br />This tag will be rendered in a strikethrough text.</li><li>U  <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li></ul><h2>Phrase elements</h2><p>The table below lists assorted Phrase elements:</p><table class="wrapped" style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Tag <br />name </th><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Function </th></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> EM</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Indicates emphasis.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> STRONG</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Indicates stronger emphasis.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> CITE</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Contains a citation or a <br />reference to other sources.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>DFN </strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><p>Indicates that this is the defining <br />instance of the enclosed term.</p></td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>CODE</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Designates a fragment of computer <br />code.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>SAMP</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Designates a sample output from <br />programs, scripts, etc.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>KBD</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates the text to be entered <br />by the user.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>VAR</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an instance of a variable <br />or program argument.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>ABBR</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an abbreviated form such <br />as WWW, HTTP, URI, and Mass.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>ACRONYM</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an acronym such as <br />WAC and radar.</td></tr></tbody></table><ul><li><p><em>EM</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>STRONG</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;b&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>CITE</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>DFN</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>CODE</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>SAMP</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>KBD</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>VAR</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>ABBR</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as normal text.</p></li><li><p><em>ACRONYM</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as normal text.</p></li></ul><h2>Ordered and unordered lists and list Item tags</h2><p>Ordered and unordered lists are rendered in an identical manner; however, ordered list items are numbered.</p><p>The report engine supports both unordered and ordered lists without attributes. The list tag attributes will be ignored in the report output. The list tag attributes are type, start, value, and compact.</p><p>Neither unordered nor ordered lists are supported in <em>XLSX</em> and <em>ODS</em> templates.</p><h3>Ordered lists</h3><p>An Ordered List is defined by the &lt;OL&gt; element. This element contains one or more &lt;LI&gt; elements that define the actual items of the list.</p><p>Unlike unordered lists (UL), items in an ordered list have a definite sequence. A conversion will render each item in the list with a number. All &lt;OL&gt; attributes will be ignored in the report output. An example of an Ordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Ordered List Tag" ac:alt="Sample of Ordered List Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ordered list tag sample.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html14 - OL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Nested ordered lists</h3><p>HTML conversion will indent nested lists with respect to the current level of nesting. Each level will begin at 1. An example of the Nested Ordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Nested OL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Nested OL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedOL1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> As shown in the figure below, the outputs in<em> RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html18 - NestedOL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p><h3>Unordered lists</h3><p>An Unordered List is defined by the UL element. This element contains one or more LI elements that define the actual list items.</p><p>A conversion will render the UL element with a bullet preceding each list item. All UL attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of an Unordered List tag:</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Sample of UL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of UL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Unordered list tag sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br />As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em>, or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><h3 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html06 - UL2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h3><h3><br />Nested unordered lists</h3><p>Lists can also be nested. <em>HTML</em> conversion will indent nested lists with respect to the current level of nesting.</p><p><em>HTML</em> conversion should attempt to present a small filled-in circle to the first level, a small circle outline to the second level, and a filled-in square to the third level. Bullets after the third level are filled-in squares.  An example of the Nested Unordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Nested UL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Nested UL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUL1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in<em> RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html10 - NestedUL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />  </p><h2>Definition list tags</h2><p>A Definition List is defined by the DL element. An entry in the list is created using the DT element for the term being defined and the DD element for the definition of the term.</p><p>A definition list can have multiple terms for a given definition, as well as multiple definitions for a given term. Authors can also give a term without a corresponding definition, and vice versa, but such a structure rarely makes sense.</p><p>A conversion will render DT as a non-indent item and DD as a single indent item. The Definition List tag is not supported in the<em> XLSX</em> template. The figure below shows an example of a Definition List tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of DL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of DL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sample DL tags.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><h2><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html22 - DL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br />Line and paragraph tags</h2><p>A line break is defined by the &lt;BR&gt; element. This element inserts a single line break. It is an empty tag, meaning that it has no end tag. The line attributes will be ignored in the report output.</p><p>A paragraph is defined by the &lt;P&gt; element. The element automatically creates some space before and after itself. The paragraph attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of a Line Break and Paragraph tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Line and Paragraph Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Line and Paragraph Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html25 - LineAndParagraph1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html26 - LineAndParagraph2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Preformatted text</h2><p>A preformatted text is defined by the &lt;PRE&gt; element. All the space and carriage returns are rendered exactly as you type them. The preformatted attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of the preformatted text:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Preformatted Text" ac:alt="Sample of Preformatted Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html29 - Preformatted1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF/ ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html30 - PRE2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html32 - PRE4.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Heading tags</h2><p>A heading is defined by the &lt;H1&gt;, &lt;H2&gt;, &lt;H3&gt;, &lt;H4&gt;, &lt;H5&gt;, or &lt;H6&gt; element. In this report, all heading tags will be rendered as &lt;b&gt; for<em> ODT, RTF,</em> and <em>OOXML</em> document outputs. The heading attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of Heading tags.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Heading Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Heading Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html33 - HEADING1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html34 - HEADING2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html36 - HEADING4.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Link tags</h2><p>A Link tag is defined by the &lt;A&gt; element. This element is used to create a link to another document with the href attribute. The href attribute specifies the destination of the link. Link tags are not supported in the <em>XLSX</em> template. The figure below shows an example of a Link tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Link Tag" ac:alt="Sample of Link Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html37 - LINK1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following three figures, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em>, and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html38 - LINK2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html39 - LINK3.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html40 - LINK4.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Table tags</h2><p>A table is defined by the &lt;TABLE&gt; element. A table consists of multi-dimensional data arranged in rows and columns.</p><h3>Table elements</h3><p>The &lt;TABLE&gt; element takes a number of optional attributes to provide presentational alternatives in a document. The table attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>border</li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Specifies the width in unit of the border of a table.</p><ul><li>bgcolor</li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Specifies the table background color. Applying the background color here will affect the whole table.</p><p><br /></p><p>Table elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p>The figure below shows an example of table tags:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Table Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Table Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html41 - Table1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html42 - Table2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html44 - Table4.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Border</strong></p><p>Border width in <em>HTML</em> is specified in pixels. When the table attributes are converted into <em>RTF</em>, <em>ODF</em>, or <em>OOXML</em>, 1 pixel will be equal to 1 pt.</p><p><strong>Color</strong></p><p>The attribute value type &quot;bgcolor&quot; refers to color definitions as specified in [SRGB]. A color value may be either a hexadecimal number (prefixed by a hash mark) or one of the following sixteen colors. Colors are case-insensitive.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The following table lists common Table Element Colors.</p><table class="wrapped" style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><th style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Color</th><th style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Hexadecimal <br />Code</th></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Black</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#00000000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Silver</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#C0C0C0</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Gray</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#808080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">White</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FFFFFF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Maroon</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#800000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Red</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FF0000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Purple</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#800080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Fuchsia</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FF00FF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Green</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#008000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Lime</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#00FF00</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Olive</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#808000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Yellow</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FFFF00</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Navy</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#000080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Blue</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#0000FF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Teal</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#008080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Aqua</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#00FFFF</td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Row elements</h3><p>The &lt;TR&gt; elements act as a container for a row of table cells. The &lt;TR&gt; elements must be contained within a &lt;TABLE&gt;.</p><p>&lt;TR&gt; contains &lt;TH&gt; or &lt;TD&gt; elements, which in turn contain the actual data of the table. &lt;TR&gt; takes presentational attributes for specifying the alignment of cells within the row and the row's background color. The row attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align - Specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in a row.</li><li>valign - Specifies the vertical position of a cell's content.  </li><li>bgcolor - Specifies the table background color. A background color will apply to rows only (see Color in Table Elements for more details).</li></ul><p>Row elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS</em>, and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p><strong>Align</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the alignment of data and the justification of text in a cell. Possible values are:</p><ul><li>left -  Left-flushed data/Left-justified text. This is the default value for table data.</li><li>center - Centered data/Center-justified text. This is the default value for table headers.</li><li>right - Right-flushed data/Right-justified text.</li><li>justify - Double -justified text.</li><li>char - No text alignment set.</li></ul><p><strong>Valign</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the vertical position of data within a cell. Possible values are:</p><ul><li>top: Cell data is flush with the top of a cell.</li><li>middle: Cell data is centered vertically within a cell. This is the default value.</li><li>bottom: Cell data is flush with the bottom of a cell.</li><li>baseline: No text alignment set.</li></ul><p>The figure below shows an example of TR tags:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TR Tags" ac:alt="Sample of TR Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html45 - TR1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html46 - TR2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html48 - TR4.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Cell elements</h3><p>The &lt;TD&gt; elements define a data cell in a table. &lt;TD&gt; elements are contained within a &lt;TR&gt; element (a table row). The cell attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align  <br />specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in the row. See Align in Row Elements for more details.</li><li>valign <br />specifies the vertical position of a cell's contents. See Valign in Row Elements for more details. </li><li>bgcolor <br />specifies the table background color. A background color will apply only to cells. See Color in Table Elements for more details.</li><li>rowspan <br />rows spanned by the cell</li><li>colspan <br />columns spanned by the cell</li></ul><p>Cell elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p><strong>Row span</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the number of rows spanned by the current cell. The default value of this attribute is one (&quot;1&quot;). For an <em>RTF</em> output, the result of row span (<em>*.rtf</em>) is readable only in Word on Mac, and MS Word.</p><p><strong>Column span</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the number of columns spanned by the current cell. The default value of this attribute is one (&quot;1&quot;). The figure below shows an example of a column span:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TD Tags As Column Spans" ac:alt="Sample of TD Tags As Column Spans"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html49 - TD1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html50 - TD2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html52 - TD4.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Column width<span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> </span></h3><p>Table column width can be adjusted via attributes and stylesheet. For example, a column's width can be set by its width attribute. Percentage or pixel units are allowed for each &lt;TD&gt; element. You may set table width in &lt;table&gt; tags by absolute pixel value or percentage of page ratio, then allocate width from the table to each column later.</p><div><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><p><br /></p><p>For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2f40341f-c2bc-43a3-b794-d695a88ed1c8"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Using attributes:
<table width="500px">
        <tr>
                <td width="25%">25%</td>
                <td width="75%">75%</td>
       </tr>
</table>
<table width="500px">
        <tr>
                <td width="400px">400px</td>
                <td width="100px">100px</td>
        </tr>
</table> 


<table>
	<tr>
		<td width="30">30px</td>
		<td width="60">60px</td>
		<td width="90">90px</td>
	</tr>
</table>
 
<table>
	<tr>
		<td width="25%">25%</td>
		<td width="75%">75%</td>
	</tr>
</table>



Using Stylesheet:
<table style="width:50%">

	<tr>
		<td style="width:70%">70%</td>
		<td style="width:30%">30%</td>
	</tr>
</table>

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div></div><div><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><p><br /></p></div><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">DOCX, RTF and ODT templates retain their width values. </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">The column width in the report is the same as defined in the Advanced HTML Editor dialog.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">If the defined values exceed the paper margins, the table column widths will be converted to a percentage format so that the table appears within the page margins.</span></p></div></div><h3>Header elements</h3><p>The &lt;TH&gt; elements define a header cell in a table. &lt;TH&gt; elements are contained within a &lt;TR&gt; element (a table row). The header attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align <br />specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in the row. See Align in Row Elements for more details.</li><li>valign <br />specifies the vertical position of a cell's contents. See Valign in Row Elements for more details. </li><li>bgcolor <br />specifies the table background color. A background color will apply to the whole table. See Color in Table Elements for more details.</li><li>rowspan <br />rows spanned by the cell. See Row span in Cell Elements for more details.</li><li>colspan <br />columns spanned by the cell. See Column span in Cell Elements for more details.</li></ul><p>The default alignment for &lt;TH&gt; is center and the default font style for &lt;TH&gt; is bold. Header elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates. The figure below shows an example of header elements:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TH Tags As Header Elements" ac:alt="Sample of TH Tags As Header Elements"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html53 - TH1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The following two figures show the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML,</em> respectively.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html54 - TH2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html56 - TH4.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Image tags</h2><p>The &lt;img&gt; tag embeds an image in a document. The &lt;img&gt; tag consists of four attributes: <strong>src</strong>, <strong>width</strong>, <strong>height</strong>, and<strong> alt</strong>.</p><h3>src</h3><p>The src attribute specifies the location of an image resource. The value of this attribute can be one of the following types:</p><ul><li>A URL. The recognized scheme types are <em>HTTP, HTTPS</em>, and <em>FILE</em></li><li>An absolute path such as<em> c:/user/image.png</em>. The path separator can be either /(slash) or \(blackslash) </li></ul><p>The output image format will depend on the format of the image source.</p><h3>width</h3><p>The width attribute specifies the width of an image in pixel units. For example, width = “100” or width = “100px”</p><h3>height</h3><p>The <strong>height</strong> attribute specifies the height of an image in pixel units. For example, height = “100”, or height = “100px”</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="629bf1ba-19df-4333-8d08-5f0d2dd35c2a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the width or height attribute of an image is not specified, the size of the image will be calculated according to the following rules:</p><ul><li>For an image file that contains the width and height properties, such as <em>JPG, PNG,</em> and <em>GIF</em>, the size of the image output will be calculated from the size of the image. </li><li>For an image file that has no width and height properties, such as <em>SVG, EMF,</em> and <em>WMF</em>, the size of the image output will be calculated from the size of the paper.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>alt</h3><p>The <strong>alt</strong> attribute specifies the alternate text of an image in case the image cannot be displayed.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f6493199-6b6b-485a-af51-db66ad772ff9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul class="alternate"><li>The alternate text of image tags in DOCX, XLSX, and PPTX is available only in Microsoft Office.</li><li>The alternate text of image tags in RTF files is shown only in the Description field, rather than the Title field in Microsoft Word and OpenOffice Writer.</li><li>The alternate text of image tags in RTF files is not supported in LibreOffice.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>In the event that the HTML code is as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8a88872e-6b09-49ee-8137-117fa047e987"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<img src="file:/D:/Waterlilies.jpg" width="200" height="200" alt="Waterlilies"/>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>The image will appear as shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of IMG Tag" ac:alt="Sample of IMG Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_img_2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Superscript and subscript tags</h2><h3>Superscript</h3><p>The &lt;sup&gt; tag defines a supersript, which is a very small letter, figure, or symbol, and printed above the line. Superscript characters can be used to write footnotes, like www<sup>[1]</sup>, for example:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sample of superscript.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Superscript output.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Subscript</h3><p>The &lt;sub&gt; tag defines a subscript, which is a very small letter, figure, or symbol, and printed below the line. For example, subscript characters can be used to write chemical formulas, such as H2O:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sample of subscript.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Output of subscript.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729805 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04075: (2026x) Supported scripting languages

- page_id: `272729805`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729805/2026x+Supported+scripting+languages
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:35:46.692+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Macro engine
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Macro Engine supports five scripting languages: BeanShell, JavaScript Rhino, Jython, Groovy, and JRuby.

**BeanShell** 
BeanShell is a lightweight scripting language for Java. The shipped version is 2.1.7. The advantage of using BeanShell is that its syntax is compatible with Java; therefore, you can use the code assistant feature in most Java IDE. The BeanShell syntax documentation is available at *[http://www.beanshell.org/docs.html](http://www.beanshell.org/docs.html)*.

**JavaScript** 
JavaScript is a scripting language based on Java syntax. The documentation is available at: 
*[http://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/scripting/nashorn/index.html](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/scripting/nashorn/index.html)* and *[https://developer.mozilla.org/en/Rhino_documentation](https://developer.mozilla.org/en/Rhino_documentation)*.

#### WARNING: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

The JavaScript Nashorn language has been removed from the modeling tool. Please use JavaScript Rhino instead. Learn more on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn' space=''] page.

**Jython** 
Jython, a successor of JPython, is a Python programming language implemented in Java. The documentation is available at *[http://wiki.python.org/jython](http://wiki.python.org/jython)*. Besides automating the modeling tool by using Jython scripting, the plugin development in the Jython programming language is also supported by the modeling tool. For more information on Jython, see JPython Scripting in the OpenAPI User Guide.

**Groovy** 
Groovy is an agile and dynamic language for the Java Virtual Machine. Macro Engine uses Groovy 2.0.1. The advantage of using Groovy is that its Java-like syntax seamlessly integrates with the existing Java source code and libraries. It supports many IDEs that provide code completion and debugging. BeanShell scripts can be easily moved to Groovy, with some modifications. For more information on Groovy, go to *[http://groovy-lang.org/](http://groovy-lang.org/)*.

**JRuby** 
JRuby is a 100% pure-Java implementation of the Ruby programming language. The documentation is available at *[http://jruby.org/documentation](http://jruby.org/documentation)*.

By default, JRuby is not included in the modeling tool, however, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Adding JRuby and installing Gems' space=''].

**Related pages**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729858 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04076: (2026x) Supported SQL statements

- page_id: `272729858`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729858/2026x+Supported+SQL+statements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:35:53.050+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Data-related modeling > (2026x) Database support > (2026x) Database code engineering
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section lists the SQL statements supported in the Cameo Data Modeler plugin. They are parsed and mapped into model constructs.

The following table provides SQL2 SQL schema statements and their support status in the Cameo Data Modeler plugin**. Yes**indicates that a statement can be generated into a DLL script from model constructs and reverse engineered from a script into model constructs.

| SQL schema statement | Supported | (Yes/No) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SQL schema definition statement | Schema definition | Yes |
| Table definition | Yes |  |
| View definition | Yes |  |
| Alter table statement | Yes |  |
| Grant statement | No |  |
| Domain definition | No |  |
| Assertion definition | No |  |
| Character set definition | No |  |
| Collation definition | No |  |
| Translation definition | No |  |
| SQL schema manipulation statement | Drop table statement | Yes |
| Drop view statement | Yes |  |
| Revoke statement | No |  |
| Alter domain statement | No |  |
| Drop assertion statement | No |  |
| Drop domain statement | No |  |
| Drop character set statement | No |  |
| Drop collation statement | No |  |
| Drop translation statement | No |  |

Some SQL schema statements (e.g. schema definition, table definition) allow implicit catalog names and unqualified schema names. In addition to SQL schema statements, the following SQL session statements must be supported:

- Set catalog statement - sets the current default catalog name.
- Set schema statement - sets the current default unqualified schema name.

Cameo Data Modeler supports the following widely used dialect statements that are not part of SQL2:

- Database definition statement (CREATE DATABASE) that creates a database
- Index statements (CREATE INDEX, DROP INDEX) that create or remove an index on a table
- Trigger statements (CREATE TRIGGER, DROP TRIGGER) that create or remove trigger(s) on a table

The following table provides details on mapping on the supported SQL schema manipulation statements into SQL constructs.

| DDL Statement or Concept | Action, model Item | Description | Visible |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Alter table statement | Modify class | Elements: table name and alter table action. Alter table action – one of: add column, add table constraint, alter column, drop table constraint, drop column. | Yes |
| Add column definition | Define attribute | Elements: column definition. | Yes |
| Add table constraint definition | Define method | Elements: table constraint definition. | Yes |
| Alter column definition | Modify attribute | Elements: mandatory column name, default clause (for add default statement only). | Yes |
| Drop table constraint definition | Delete method | Elements: constraint name, drop behavior | Yes |
| Drop column definition | Delete attribute | Elements: column name, drop behavior | Yes |
| Drop schema statement | Delete package | Elements: schema name, drop behavior | Yes |
| Drop table statement | Delete class | Elements: table name, drop behavior | Yes |
| Drop view statement | Delete class | Elements: table name, drop behavior | Yes |
| Drop behavior | Action property | Modifiers: CASCADE, RESTRICT | No |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

They are parsed and mapped into model constructs.</p><p>The following table provides SQL2 SQL schema statements and their support status in the Cameo Data Modeler plugin<strong>. In addition to SQL schema statements, the following SQL session statements must be supported:</p><ul><li>Set catalog statement - sets the current default catalog name.</li><li>Set schema statement - sets the current default unqualified schema name.</li></ul><p>Cameo Data Modeler supports the following widely used dialect statements that are not part of SQL2:</p><ul><li>Database definition statement (CREATE DATABASE) that creates a database</li><li>Index statements (CREATE INDEX, DROP INDEX) that create or remove an index on a table</li><li>Trigger statements (CREATE TRIGGER, DROP TRIGGER) that create or remove trigger(s) on a table</li></ul><p>The following table provides details on mapping on the supported SQL schema manipulation statements into SQL constructs.</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 99.9451%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 20.8027%;" /><col style="width: 14.9844%;" /><col style="width: 54.9359%;" /><col style="width: 9.27695%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p><strong>DDL Statement or Concept</strong></p></th><th><p><strong>Action, model Item<br /></strong></p><p><br /></p></th><th><p><strong>Description</strong></p></th><th><p><strong>Visible</strong></p></th></tr><tr><td><strong>Alter table statement</strong></td><td>Modify class</td><td>Elements: table name and alter table action. Alter table action – one of: add column, add table constraint, alter column, drop table constraint, drop column.</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Add column definition</strong></td><td>Define attribute</td><td>Elements: column definition.</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Add table constraint definition</strong></p></td><td>Define method</td><td>Elements: table constraint definition.</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Alter column definition</strong></td><td>Modify attribute</td><td>Elements: mandatory column name, default clause (for add default statement only).</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Drop table constraint definition</strong></p></td><td>Delete method</td><td>Elements: constraint name, drop behavior</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Drop column definition </strong></td><td>Delete attribute</td><td>Elements: column name, drop behavior</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Drop schema statement</strong></td><td>Delete package</td><td>Elements: schema name, drop behavior</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Drop table statement</strong></td><td>Delete class</td><td>Elements: table name, drop behavior</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Drop view statement</strong></td><td>Delete class</td><td>Elements: table name, drop behavior</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Drop behavior</strong></td><td>Action property</td><td>Modifiers: CASCADE, RESTRICT</td><td>No</td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

## PAGE 04079: (2026x) Supports relationship between Operational Activities and Services in UAF 1.2

- page_id: `272738773`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738773/2026x+Supports+relationship+between+Operational+Activities+and+Services+in+UAF+1.2
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:50:34.051+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Migration User Guides > (2026x) UPDM projects migration manual > (2026x) UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.2 > (2026x) UPDM 2.1 to UAF 1.2. Mapping issues and solutions
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

After the migration to UAF, the Supports relationships between Operational Activities and Services are created. The relationships are displayed in the following matrices:

UAF: Services to Operational Activities Mapping Matrix

DoDAF: SvcV-5 Services to Operational Activity Traceability Matrix

NAF: NSOV-3 Services to Operational Activities Mapping

NAF4: S4 Services to Operational Activities Mapping

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272735239 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04080: (2026x) Suspect Links

- page_id: `272735239`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272735239/2026x+Suspect+Links
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:45:08.680+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Model analysis
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

Working on a server project requires up-to-date knowledge of all relevant project changes. Thus, we introduce a new set of validation rules for suspect link analysis. These rules help you track changes in Requirements that are linked to design elements (such as SysML v1 Blocks, Test Cases, Use Cases, etc.) by Satisfy, Derive, Refine or Verify relationships, and stay informed about unlinked and deprecated Requirements in the model.

700500

INLINE

The following topics contain more information on Requirements tracking:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Suspect links tracking options' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Suspect links tracking options' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes' space='']

You can visit the page[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Suspect links semantics' space='']to learn more about the topic.

You can also create your own suspect links customization to keep track of the changes in the elements of the specified type. To learn more, read the following topic:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Customizing suspect links tracking' space='']

/><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Tracking unlinked Requirements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><p><ac:link ac:anchor="Solving untracked links issue"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Solving untracked links issue]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></li></ul><p><span>You can visit the page </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Suspect links semantics" /></ac:link><span> to learn more about the topic.</span></p><p>You can also create your own suspect links customization to keep track of the changes in the elements of the specified type. To learn more, read the following topic:</p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Customizing suspect links tracking" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272737304 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04081: (2026x) Suspect links semantics

- page_id: `272737304`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272737304/2026x+Suspect+links+semantics
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:48:29.639+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Model analysis > (2026x) Suspect Links
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Enabling suspect links tracking applies the *<<track>>* stereotype to the relationships. It is applied to the relationships automatically right after enabling the suspect links tracking, changing the tracking scope, or creating a new relationship (in the case when suspect links are already enabled) if the following criteria are satisfied:

- The relationship type is Satisfy, Verify, Refine, or deriveReq. If custom suspect links tracking is created, the relationship type, direction, source, and target element types should be as defined in the configuration.
- The relationship itself and the relationship's source and target elements are under the specified tracking scope.

The stereotype contains *fingerprint* and *version* tags:

- fingerprint: is used to indicate that changes occurred at the end of a relationship. In other words, the fingerprint is used to highlight that relationship is suspected.
- version: is used to get the Teamwork Cloud server project version, which should be compared to the latest in order to identify what changes occurred in the linked element . In other words, the version is used when the action "Show Changes" is performed on a suspected link.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Upon enabling suspect links tracking, the stereotype *<<track>>*, which contains fingerprint and version tags, is applied tothe relationship 'Satisfy'.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Enabling suspect links tracking applies the <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">&lt;&lt;track&gt;&gt;</em> stereotype to the relationships. It is applied to the relationships automatically right after enabling the suspect links tracking, changing the tracking scope, or creating a new relationship (in the case when suspect links are already enabled) if the following criteria are satisfied:</p><ul><li>The relationship type is Satisfy, Verify, Refine, or deriveReq. If custom suspect links tracking is created, the relationship type, direction, source, and target element types should be as defined in the configuration.</li><li>The relationship itself and the relationship's source and target elements are under the specified tracking scope.</li></ul><p>The stereotype contains <em>fingerprint</em> and <em>version</em> tags:</p><ul><li>fingerprint: is used to indicate<span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> that changes occurred at the end of a relationship. In other words, the fingerprint is used to highlight that relationship is suspected.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">version: </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">is used to get the Teamwork Cloud server project version, which should be compared to the latest in order to identify what changes occurred in the linked element</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">. <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In other words, t</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">he version is used when the action &quot;Show Changes&quot; is performed on a suspected link.</span></span></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-bottom: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="suspect_links_semantics.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Suspect links semantics" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br />Upon enabling suspect links tracking, the stereotype <em>&lt;&lt;track&gt;&gt;</em>, which contains fingerprint and version tags, <span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">is applied to </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">the relationship 'Satisfy'</span>.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272737291 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04082: (2026x) Suspect links tracking options

- page_id: `272737291`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272737291/2026x+Suspect+links+tracking+options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:48:27.423+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Model analysis > (2026x) Suspect Links
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

767157416

767157437

767157414

**On this page**

**3**

767157427

##### Tracking Deprecated Requirements

Suspect links analysis allows tracking deprecated Requirements. This is useful when you are working with Requirements and some of them are no longer used. In that case, apply the *«deprecated»* stereotype to mark the Requirements of this type. All deprecated Requirements are provided in the **Validation Results** panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To check whether deprecated Requirements are tracked

- In the main menu, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options . If the Track Deprecated Elements option is set to true , tracking deprecated elements is enabled. [ATTACHMENT filename='track_deprecated_elements_option.png']

##### Tracking Unlinked Requirements

Suspect links analysis allows tracking unlinked Requirements. This is useful when you're working with structure elements and you need to know which Requirements remain unsatisfied.

To check whether unlinked Requirements are satisfied

1. In the main toolbar, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options .
2. Click the arrow next to the Track Unlinked Requirements option and choose: a) All – if you want to track all the unlinked Requirements b) Excluding Owning – if you want to track only those unlinked Requirements, that have no sub-Requirements c) None – if you want to disable tracking unlinked Requirements. [ATTACHMENT filename='excluding_owning.png']
3. Click OK .

All unlinked Requirements are provided in the **Validation Results** panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Tracking Links in Main Project Only

You can limit the suspect links analysis scope to links contained in the main project only. If the option is enabled, used projects are excluded from the tracking scope.

To track links in the main project only

- In the main menu, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options . If the Track Links in Main Project Only option is set to true , links are tracked in the main project only. [ATTACHMENT filename='track_links_in_main_project_only.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272735779 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04083: (2026x) SV-1 Systems Interface Description

- page_id: `272735779`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272735779/2026x+SV-1+Systems+Interface+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:45:52.562+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) DoDAF 2.0 > (2026x) Systems viewpoint
- labels: ['sv-1-systems-interface-description', 'sv-1', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

404622161

404622164

404622162

**Description**

The SV-1 addresses the composition and interaction of systems. For DoDAF V2.0, the SV-1 incorporates the human elements as types of performers- Organizations and Personnel Types.

The SV-1 links together the operational and systems architecture models by depicting how resources are structured and interact to realize the logical architecture specified in an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description' space='']. A SV-1 may represent the realization of a requirement specified in an OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description (i.e., in a “To-Be” architecture), and so there may be many alternative SV models that could realize the operational requirement. Alternatively, in an “As-Is” architecture, the OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description may simply be a simplified, logical representation of the SV-1 to allow communication of key resource flows to non-technical stakeholders.

A system resource flow is a simplified representation of a pathway or network pattern, usually depicted graphically as a connector (i.e., a line with possible amplifying information). The SV-1 depicts all system resource flows between systems that are of interest. Note that resource flows between systems may be further specified in detail in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-2 Systems Resource Flow Description' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-6 Systems Resource Flow Matrix' space=''].

Sub-system assemblies may be identified in SV-1 to any level (i.e., depth) of decomposition the architect sees fit. SV-1 may also identify the Physical Assets (e.g., Platforms) at which resources are deployed, and optionally overlay Operational Activities and Locations that utilize those resources. In many cases, an Operational Activity and Locations depicted in an OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description model may well be the logical representation of the resource that is shown in SV-1.

The intended usage of the SV-1 includes:

- Definition of system concepts.
- Definition of system options.
- System resource flow requirements capture.
- Capability integration planning.
- System integration management.
- Operational planning (capability and performer definition).

The SV-1 is used in two complementary ways:

- Describe the resource flows exchanged between resources in the architecture.
- Describe a solution, or solution option, in terms of the components of capability and their physical integration on platforms and other facilities.

**Implementation**

SV-1 can be represented using:

- SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- UML Composite Structure diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.
- SysML Internal Block diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

An SV-1 can optionally be adorned with performersoriginally specified in an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description' space='']. In this way, traceability can be established from the logical OV structure to the physical SV structure.

An interaction, as depicted in the SV-1, is an indicator that information passes from one performerto another. In the case of systems, this can be expanded into further detail in an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-2 Systems Resource Flow Description' space='']. Resource Flows are summarized in a Systems-Systems Matrix ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-3 Systems-Systems Matrix' space='']).

The system Functions performed by the performersare specified in an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description' space=''], but may optionally be overlaid in the SV-1.

An Operational View (OV) suite may specify a set of requirements – either as a specific operational plan, or a scenario for procurement. As [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-5 Operational Activity Model' space=''] specify the logical structure and behavior, SV-1 and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description' space='']specify the physical structure and behavior (to the level of detail required by the architectural stakeholders).

404622159

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Natural Resource' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Architecture' space='MED']

/></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272733431 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04084: (2026x) SV-1 Systems Interface Internal Description

- page_id: `272733431`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272733431/2026x+SV-1+Systems+Interface+Internal+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:42:26.524+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) DoDAF 2.0 > (2026x) Systems viewpoint
- labels: ['sv-1-systems-interface-internal-description', 'sv-1-internal', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

404838096

404838098

404838097

**Description**

The SV-1 addresses the internal composition and interaction of systems.

**Implementation**

SV-1 can be represented using:

- SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- UML Composite Structure diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.
- SysML Internal Block diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

An SV-1 can optionally be adorned with performers originally specified in an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description' space='']. In this way, traceability can be established from the logical OV structure to the physical SV structure.

An interaction, as depicted in the SV-1, is an indicator that information passes from one performer to another. In the case of systems, this can be expanded into further detail in an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-2 Systems Resource Flow Description' space='']. Resource flows are summarized in a Systems-Systems Matrix ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-3 Systems-Systems Matrix' space='']).

Thesystem Functions performed by the Nodesare specified in an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description' space=''], but may optionally be overlaid in the SV-1.

An Operational View (OV) suite may specify a set of requirements – either as a specific operational plan, or a scenario for procurement. As [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-5 Operational Activity Model' space=''] specify the logical structure and behavior, SV-1 and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description' space=''] specify the physical structure and behavior (to the level of detail required by the architectural stakeholders).

404838095

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Role' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Port' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Connector' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Exchange' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating SV-1 Internal diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating Resource Exchange in SV-1 Internal diagram' space='']

In this way, traceability can be established from the logical OV structure to the physical SV structure.</p><p>An interaction, as depicted in the SV-1, is an indicator that information passes from one performer to another. In the case of systems, this can be expanded into further detail in an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SV-2 Systems Resource Flow Description" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SV-2]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. Resource flows are summarized in a Systems-Systems Matrix (<ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SV-3 Systems-Systems Matrix" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SV-3]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>).</p><p>The<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> system Functions</span> <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">performed</span> by the <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Nodes </span>are specified in an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SV-4 System Functionality Description]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, but may optionally be overlaid in the SV-1.</p><p>An Operational View (OV) suite may specify a set of requirements – either as a specific operational plan, or a scenario for procurement. As <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[OV-2]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) OV-5 Operational Activity Model" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[OV-5]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> specify the logical structure and behavior, SV-1 and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SV-4]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> specify the physical structure and behavior (to the level of detail required by the architectural stakeholders).</p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3959acf7-850d-4cf6-b541-1391f37bafd1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">404838095</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Resource Role" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Resource Port" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Resource Connector" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Resource Exchange" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating SV-1 Internal diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating Resource Exchange in SV-1 Internal diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 04085: (2026x) SV-10a Resource Parametric

- page_id: `272731157`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272731157/2026x+SV-10a+Resource+Parametric
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:38:27.234+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Systems Viewpoint > (2026x) MODAF. SV-10a Resource Constraints Specification
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

497880528

497880530

497880529

**Description**

A Parametric diagram includes the usage of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Block' space='MED'] to constrain the properties of another Capability Configuration. It contains [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Property' space='MED'] and constraint parameters as well as other properties from within that internal block context. All properties displayed, other than the constraints themselves, must either be bound directly to a constraint parameter or contain a property that is bound to a constraint parameter (through any number of containment levels). A constraint block generally contain many constraints, each of them containing many constraint parameters.

497880527

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Working with Parametric diagram' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272731752 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04087: (2026x) SV-10a Systems Rules Model

- page_id: `272731752`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272731752/2026x+SV-10a+Systems+Rules+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:39:37.412+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) DoDAF 2.0 > (2026x) Systems viewpoint
- labels: ['sv-10a-systems-rules-model', 'sv-10a', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

408324973

408324976

408324974

**Description**

The SV-10a specifies functional and nonfunctional constraints on the implementation aspects of the architecture (i.e., the structural and behavioral elements of the Systems Viewpoint).

The SV-10a DoDAF-described Model describes constraints on the resources, functions, data, and ports that make up the SV. The constraints are specified in text and may be functional or structural (i.e., non-functional).

The intended usage of the SV-10a includes:

- Definition of implementation logic.
- Identification of resource constraints.

Implementation

SV-10a can be represented using:

- SV-10a table. [ATTACHMENT filename='sv_10a.png']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-10a Systems Parametric' space=''] .
- SV-10a spreadsheet report.

In contrast to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) OV-6a Operational Rules Model' space=''], SV-10a focuses on physical and data constraints rather than business rules.

**Constraint owner**

You can choose where all the constraints will be stored through the**Project Options**.

To select a constraint owner

1. In the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Go to General > UAF , and in the Default Constraint Owners property group, choose a needed owner.

408324971

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Constraint' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Artifact' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Software' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Capability Configuration' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Organization' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Post' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Function' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Exchange' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Information' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating SV-10a table' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-10a Systems Parametric' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272733441 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04088: (2026x) SV-10b Systems State Transition Description

- page_id: `272733441`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272733441/2026x+SV-10b+Systems+State+Transition+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:42:27.828+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) DoDAF 2.0 > (2026x) Systems viewpoint
- labels: ['sv-10b-systems-state-transition-description', 'sv-10b', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The SV-10b is a graphical method of describing a resourceor system function response to various events by changing its state. The diagram basically represents the sets of events to which the resources in the Activities respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action.

The explicit time sequencing of service functions in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in SV-4 Systems Functionality Description. The SV-10b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the functions. Alternatively, SV-10b can be used to reflect explicit sequencing of the actions internal to a single function, or the sequencing of system functions with respect to a specific resource.

The intended usage of the SV-10b includes:

- Definition of states, events and state transitions (behavioral modeling).
- Identification of constraints.

**Implementation**

SV-10b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The SV-10b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of functions described in SV-4 Systems Functionality Description. However, the relationship between the actions included in SV-10b and the functions in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description' space=''] depends on the purposes of the architecture and the level of abstraction used in the models. The explicit sequencing of functions in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description' space='']. SV-10b can be used to reflect explicit sequencing of the functions, the sequencing of actions internal to a single function, or the sequencing of functions with respect to a specific resource.

Depending upon the architecture project’s needs, the SV-10b may be used separately or in conjunction with the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-10c Systems Event-Trace Description' space=''].

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Artifact' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Software' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Capability Configuration' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Organization' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Post' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The SV-10b is a graphical method of describing a resource<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> or system function</span> response to various events by changing its state. The diagram basically represents the sets of events to which the resources in the Activities respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action.</p><p>The explicit time sequencing of service functions in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in SV-4 Systems Functionality Description. The SV-10b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the functions. Alternatively, SV-10b can be used to reflect explicit sequencing of the actions internal to a single function, or the sequencing of system functions with respect to a specific resource.</p><p>The intended usage of the SV-10b includes:</p><ul><li data-uuid="ac06112c-ebdd-4c0d-94e7-df03c805edf9">Definition of states, events and state transitions (behavioral modeling).</li><li data-uuid="eda93feb-6a0c-49a6-b15a-38de62fc94f4">Identification of constraints.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SV-10b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="SV-10b Systems State Transition Description" ac:alt="SV-10b Systems State Transition Description"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SV-10b_sys_state_transit_desc.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SV-10b Systems State Transition Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The SV-10b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of functions described in SV-4 Systems Functionality Description. However, the relationship between the actions included in SV-10b and the functions in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description" /></ac:link> depends on the purposes of the architecture and the level of abstraction used in the models. The explicit sequencing of functions in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description" /></ac:link>. SV-10b can be used to reflect explicit sequencing of the functions, the sequencing of actions internal to a single function, or the sequencing of functions with respect to a specific resource.</p><p>Depending upon the architecture project’s needs, the SV-10b may be used separately or in conjunction with the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SV-10c Systems Event-Trace Description" /></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="a141d2dd-583e-48e3-9f86-b50f3d84ae01"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="State" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="e2df8941-7305-43a5-a30a-9b7f94743ec6"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Resource Artifact" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="9a52e886-9a8d-49f3-83e4-58ff7900f427"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Software" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="be7b5231-2714-41ec-890e-df126a26a4a7"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Capability Configuration" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="df43132f-9b89-404b-ac93-ce6428e2e201"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Organization" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="54471024-edec-485c-87ef-86d1ff94dbe0"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Post" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="7fc137f1-63cf-4b1a-a416-598f1225d1ea"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272731765 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04089: (2026x) SV-10c Systems Event-Trace Description

- page_id: `272731765`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272731765/2026x+SV-10c+Systems+Event-Trace+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:39:39.170+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) DoDAF 2.0 > (2026x) Systems viewpoint
- labels: ['sv-10c-systems-event-trace-description', 'sv-10c', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

408519862

408519864

408519863

**Description**

The SV-10c provides a time-ordered examination of the interactions between functional resources. Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation.

The SV-10c is valuable for moving to the next level of detail from the initial solution design, to help define a sequence of functions and system data interfaces, and to ensure that each participating resource or system port role has the necessary information it needs, at the right time, to perform its assigned functionality.

The intended usage of the SV-10c includes:

- Analysis of resource events impacting operation.
- Behavioral analysis.
- Identification of non-functional system requirements.

**Implementation**

SV-10b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The SV-10c is typically used in conjunction with the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-10b Systems State Transition Description' space=''] to describe the dynamic behavior of resources. The data content of messages that connect resource flows in a SV-10c may be related with resource flows (the interactions in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-1 Systems Interface Description' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-3 Systems-Systems Matrix' space='']), resource flows (the data in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-4 Systems Functionality Description' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SV-6 Systems Resource Flow Matrix' space='']) and entities (in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) DIV-3 Physical Data Model' space='']) modeled in other models.

408519861

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Message' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Exchange' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
