The definition list supports one to multiple terms and the corresponding definition(s). The term comes before the definition. <br />The definition list element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Definitions List" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Definitions List"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of definition list.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported HTML lists" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Definitions List" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Definitions List"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of definition list.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported HTML lists" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729394 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04176: (2026x) The supported HTML table elements

- page_id: `272729394`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729394/2026x+The+supported+HTML+table+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:35:00.679+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The HTML table element (<table>) is divided into rows (<tr>), and each row may contain a number of table data cells (<td>) or (<th>).

The <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th> elements support two attributes: (i) align and (ii) valign.

**(i) align**

You use align to horizontally arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:

- "left" to left-align the content in a cell.
- "center" to center-align the content in a cell.
- “right” to right-align the content in a cell.
- “justify” to stretch the content with equal width.
- "char" to align the content to a specific character.

**(ii) valign**

You use valign to vertically arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:

- "top" to top-align the content in a cell.
- "middle" to center-align the content in a cell.
- "bottom" to bottom-align the content in a cell.

The table element shown in the figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The HTML table element (&lt;table&gt;) is divided into rows (&lt;tr&gt;), and each row may contain a number of table data cells (&lt;td&gt;) or (&lt;th&gt;).</p><p>The &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt; elements support two attributes: (i) align and (ii) valign.</p><p><strong>(i) align</strong></p><p>You use align to horizontally arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:</p><ul><li>&quot;left&quot; to left-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;center&quot; to center-align the content in a cell.</li><li>“right” to right-align the content in a cell.</li><li>“justify” to stretch the content with equal width.</li><li>&quot;char&quot; to align the content to a specific character.</li></ul><p><strong>(ii) valign</strong></p><p>You use valign to vertically arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:</p><ul><li>&quot;top&quot; to top-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;middle&quot; to center-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;bottom&quot; to bottom-align the content in a cell.</li></ul><p>The table element shown in the figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Table" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Table"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of table.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported HTML table elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Table" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Table"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of table.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported HTML table elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729330 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04177: (2026x) The Supported HTML Tags

- page_id: `272729330`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729330/2026x+The+Supported+HTML+Tags
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:34:50.439+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### HTML Paragraph Elements

The HTML tags in the Report Wizard’s engine for DocBook defines how a paragraph is arranged in a DocBook document. There are two categories of paragraph elements:

1.1.1 Paragraph 
1.1.2 Preformatted Text

##### Paragraph

The Paragraph element (<p>) defines a paragraph. The <p> element shown in the first figure will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Preformatted Text

The Preformatted text element (<pre>) displays the text exactly as you expected it to. The <pre> tag preserves both spaces and line breaks. The <pre> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Font Styles

Report Wizard specifies a group of HTML tags that you can use to define how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called font style elements.

1. Teletype Text
2. Italics
3. Bold
4. Strikethrough
5. Underline

##### Teletype Text

The HTML Teletype Text element (<tt>) creates teletype or monospaced text. The <tt> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Italics

The HTML Italics element (<i>) renders text slanted to the right. The <i> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Bold

The HTML Bold element (<b>) renders bold text. The <b> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Strikethrough

The HTML Strikethrough element (<strike> or <s>) renders text with a horizontal strike or a line through the middle. The <strike> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Underline

The HTML Underline element (<u>) underlines text. The underline <u> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook as in second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Phrase Elements

There is a group of HTML tags that allows you to specify how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called HTML Phrase Tags or Elements. The following is a list of these font style elements:

1. Emphasis
2. Strong
3. Citation
4. Definition
5. A Fragment of Computer Code
6. Sample Text
7. Keyboard Input
8. Variable

##### Emphasis

The Emphasis element (<em>) emphasizes text content and displays it in italics. The <em> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Strong

The Strong element (<strong>) strongly emphasizes text content. It renders the text in bold. The <strong> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Citation

The Citation element (<cite>) presents a citation or a reference to other sources. The <cite> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Definition

The HTML Definition element (<dfn>) gives a definition for the term being defined. The Definition element <dfn> shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### A Fragment of Computer Code

The HTML fragment of code element (<code>) designates part of computer code. The <code> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Sample Text

The HTML Sample Text element (<samp>) designates sample output of programs or scripts. The <samp> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Keyboard Input

The HTML Keyboard Input element (<kbd>) indicates user input or text to be entered by the user. The <kbd> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Variable

The HTML Variable element (<var>) indicates an instance of a variable or program argument. The <var> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Link Element

The HTML Link element (<a>) defines a hyperlink that links one page to another. The <a> element supports the attribute href, which specifies the URL of a page the hyperlink goes to.

The <a> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Image Element

The HTML Image element (<img>) defines an image in an HTML page. The <img> element supports the attribute src, which specifies the URL of an image. 
The <img> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h2>HTML Paragraph Elements</h2><p>The HTML tags in the Report Wizard’s engine for DocBook defines how a paragraph is arranged in a DocBook document. There are two categories of paragraph elements:</p><p>1.1.1 Paragraph<br />1.1.2 Preformatted Text</p><h3>Paragraph</h3><p>The Paragraph element (&lt;p&gt;) defines a paragraph. The &lt;p&gt; element shown in the first figure will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Paragraph" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Paragraph"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for paragraph.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Paragraph" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Paragraph"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for paragraph.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Preformatted Text</h3><p>The Preformatted text element (&lt;pre&gt;) displays the text exactly as you expected it to. The &lt;pre&gt; tag preserves both spaces and line breaks. The &lt;pre&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Pre-formatted Text" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Pre-formatted Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for preformatted text.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Pre-formatted Text" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Pre-formatted Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for preformatted text.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Font Styles</h2><p>Report Wizard specifies a group of HTML tags that you can use to define how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called font style elements.</p><ol><li>Teletype Text</li><li>Italics</li><li>Bold</li><li>Strikethrough</li><li>Underline</li></ol><h3>Teletype Text</h3><p>The HTML Teletype Text element (&lt;tt&gt;) creates teletype or monospaced text. The &lt;tt&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Teletype Text" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Teletype Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for teletype text.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Teletype Text" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Teletype Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for teletype text.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Italics</h3><p>The HTML Italics element (&lt;i&gt;) renders text slanted to the right. The &lt;i&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Italics" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Italics"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for italics.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Italics" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Italics"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for italics.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Bold</h3><p>The HTML Bold element (&lt;b&gt;) renders bold text. The &lt;b&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Bold" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Bold"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for bold.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Bold" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Bold"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for bold.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Strikethrough</h3><p>The HTML Strikethrough element (&lt;strike&gt; or &lt;s&gt;) renders text with a horizontal strike or a line through the middle. The &lt;strike&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Strikethrough" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Strikethrough"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for strikethrough.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Strikethrough" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Strikethrough"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for strikethrough.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Underline</h3><p>The HTML Underline element (&lt;u&gt;) underlines text. The underline &lt;u&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook as in second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Underline" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Underline"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for underline.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Underline" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Underline"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for underline.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Phrase Elements</h2><p>There is a group of HTML tags that allows you to specify how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called HTML Phrase Tags or Elements. The following is a list of these font style elements:</p><ol><li>Emphasis</li><li>Strong</li><li>Citation</li><li>Definition</li><li>A Fragment of Computer Code</li><li>Sample Text</li><li>Keyboard Input</li><li>Variable</li></ol><h3>Emphasis</h3><p>The Emphasis element (&lt;em&gt;) emphasizes text content and displays it in italics. The &lt;em&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Emphasis" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Emphasis"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for emphasis.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Emphasis" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Emphasis"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for emphasis.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Strong</h3><p>The Strong element (&lt;strong&gt;) strongly emphasizes text content. It renders the text in bold. The &lt;strong&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Strong" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Strong"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for strong.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Strong" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Strong"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for strong.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Citation</h3><p>The Citation element (&lt;cite&gt;) presents a citation or a reference to other sources. The &lt;cite&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Citation" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Citation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for citation.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Citation" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Citation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for citation.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Definition</h3><p>The HTML Definition element (&lt;dfn&gt;) gives a definition for the term being defined. The Definition element &lt;dfn&gt; shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for Definition" ac:alt="HTML Tag for Definition"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for definition.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for Definition" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for Definition"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for definition.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>A Fragment of Computer Code</h3><p>The HTML fragment of code element (&lt;code&gt;) designates part of computer code. The &lt;code&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Code" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Code"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of code.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Code" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Code"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of code.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Sample Text</h3><p>The HTML Sample Text element (&lt;samp&gt;) designates sample output of programs or scripts. The &lt;samp&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Sample Text" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Sample Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of sample text.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Sample Text" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Sample Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of sample text.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Keyboard Input</h3><p>The HTML Keyboard Input element (&lt;kbd&gt;) indicates user input or text to be entered by the user. The &lt;kbd&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Keyboard Input" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Keyboard Input"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of keyboard input.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Keyboard Input" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Keyboard Input"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of keyboard input.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Variable</h3><p>The HTML Variable element (&lt;var&gt;) indicates an instance of a variable or program argument. The &lt;var&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Variable" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Variable"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of variable.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Variable" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Variable"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of variable.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Link Element</h2><p>The HTML Link element (&lt;a&gt;) defines a hyperlink that links one page to another. The &lt;a&gt; element supports the attribute href, which specifies the URL of a page the hyperlink goes to.</p><p>The &lt;a&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Link" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Link"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of link.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Link" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Link"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of link.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Image Element</h2><p>The HTML Image element (&lt;img&gt;) defines an image in an HTML page. The &lt;img&gt; element supports the attribute src, which specifies the URL of an image.<br />The &lt;img&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Image" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of image.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Image" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of image.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729703 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04178: (2026x) The supported style sheet properties

- page_id: `272729703`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729703/2026x+The+supported+style+sheet+properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:35:34.483+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The DocBook engine of Report Wizard supports specific style sheets within an HTML element. The supported style sheet properties are as follows:

1. Font
2. Text Align
3. Text Decoration
4. Vertical Align

#### Font

The “font” property is a shorthand property for setting the “font-style”, and “font-weight” properties.

##### Font Style

The “font-style” property defines that the font be displayed either in normal way or in italics within a specified font family. The font-style shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Font Weight

The “font-weight” property specifies the weight of the font. The values “100” to “900” form an ordered sequence where each value indicates the font weight. The keyword ‘normal’ is synonymous with “400”, and “bold” is synonymous with “700”.

The following values are defined:

- normal, lighter, 100, 200, 300, and 400 will be rendered as ‘normal’.
- bold, bolder, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 900 will be rendered as ‘bold’.

The font-weight property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Text Align

The text-align property specifies the horizontal alignment of inline content of a block. The supported tags of the text-align property are <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th>. The values of this property are:

- "left" to left-align the content.
- "center" to center-align the content.
- “right” to right-align the content.
- “justify” to stretch the content with equal width.
- "char" to align the content to a specific character.

The text-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Text Decoration

The text-decoration property specifies decorations that are added to text. The values of this property are none, underline, or line-through. The text-decoration property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Vertical Align

The vertical-align property specifies the vertical alignment of text or an element within a lined box. The supported tags of vertical-align are <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th>. The values of the vertical-align property are top, middle, and bottom.

- "top" to top-align the content.
- "middle" to center-align the content.
- "bottom" to bottom-align the content.

The vertical-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The DocBook engine of Report Wizard supports specific style sheets within an HTML element. The supported style sheet properties are as follows:</p><ol><li>Font</li><li>Text Align</li><li>Text Decoration</li><li>Vertical Align</li></ol><h2>Font</h2><p>The “font” property is a shorthand property for setting the “font-style”, and “font-weight” properties.</p><h3>Font Style</h3><p>The “font-style” property defines that the font be displayed either in normal way or in italics within a specified font family. The font-style shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Font Style" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Font Style"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of font style.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Style" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Style"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of font style.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Font Weight</h3><p>The “font-weight” property specifies the weight of the font. The values “100” to “900” form an ordered sequence where each value indicates the font weight. The keyword ‘normal’ is synonymous with “400”, and “bold” is synonymous with “700”.</p><p>The following values are defined:</p><ul><li>normal, lighter, 100, 200, 300, and 400 will be rendered as ‘normal’.</li><li>bold, bolder, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 900 will be rendered as ‘bold’.</li></ul><p>The font-weight property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of font weight.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Weight" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Weight"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of font weight.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Text Align</h2><p>The text-align property specifies the horizontal alignment of inline content of a block. The supported tags of the text-align property are &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt;. The values of this property are:</p><ul><li>&quot;left&quot; to left-align the content.</li><li>&quot;center&quot; to center-align the content.</li><li>“right” to right-align the content.</li><li>“justify” to stretch the content with equal width.</li><li>&quot;char&quot; to align the content to a specific character.</li></ul><p>The text-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Align" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of text align.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Align" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of text align.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Text Decoration</h2><p>The text-decoration property specifies decorations that are added to text. The values of this property are none, underline, or line-through. The text-decoration property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of text decoration.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of text decoration.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Vertical Align</h2><p>The vertical-align property specifies the vertical alignment of text or an element within a lined box. The supported tags of vertical-align are &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt;. The values of the vertical-align property are top, middle, and bottom.</p><ul><li>&quot;top&quot; to top-align the content.</li><li>&quot;middle&quot; to center-align the content.</li><li>&quot;bottom&quot; to bottom-align the content.</li></ul><p>The vertical-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of vertical align.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of vertical align.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272732973 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04179: (2026x) The Use Case scenario created with the version 17.0.1 or earlier

- page_id: `272732973`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272732973/2026x+The+Use+Case+scenario+created+with+the+version+17.0.1+or+earlier
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:41:36.861+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Functional analysis > (2026x) Use Cases > (2026x) Use Case scenario
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

880408184

880408186

880408185

Beginning with version 17.0.2 of the modeling tool, Use Case scenarios of projects created with earlier versions are stored in the **Use Case Scenario Obsolete**property group in the Use Case [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification window' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- To see the Use Case Scenario Obsolete property group, change the property display mode to Expert .
- The Use Case Scenario Obsolete property group is displayed only if the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case description profile' space='MED'] is loaded.

880408183

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) State Machine diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activity diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Sequence diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Use Case diagram' space='']

ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a140a788-8f16-4910-950c-f6224de828d5"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>To see the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Use Case Scenario Obsolete </strong>property group, change the property display mode to <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Expert</strong>.</li><li>The <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Use Case Scenario Obsolete </strong>property group is displayed only if the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case description profile" ri:space-key="MED" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Use Case Description #### NOTE: Functionalities

Functionalities

This feature is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

A Time Diagram is an extension of UML notation. The time diagram is similar to a sequence diagram, but the model elements of the time diagram have predefined stereotypes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Time diagram

45928452890

INLINE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729089 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04181: (2026x) Time Periods dialog

- page_id: `272729089`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729089/2026x+Time+Periods+dialog
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:33:46.726+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Dialogs
- labels: ['time-periods-dialog', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Button Visibility

1014964889

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Button Visibility

1014964891

INLINE

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Button Visibility

1014964890

INLINE

##### Time Periods

In the UPDM plugin, time periods are used to specify resources for the architecture against a timeline indicating when they are expected to be in use. Time periods can be defined when the subjects, or resources, of the forecast are already added in the table.As shown below, the**Technology area**column must be filled in with resources**before**adding a specific time period.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Time Periods Dialog

The **Time Periods** dialog is essential when defining time periods for the **forecast of the resources**.

The **Time Periods** dialog consists of three areas:

- **Toolbar** - This area allows you to control how properties in the dialog are displayed: Categorized view, Alphabetical view, Expand all, Collapse all.
- **Properties** - Provides a list of properties that are available to specify.
- Properties value cells - The fields where you can set values for each property.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Time Periods dialog

Due to the abundance of button in the dialog, the instructions are broken down into two parts: General instructions and Specific instructions. General instructions will explain how tothe Time Periods dialog and how to approach the main part of the Time Periods dialog: the. Specific instructions will explain how tospecify [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Forecast Kinds in Time Periods dialog' space=''] in the dialog.

openTo open the **Time Periods** dialog

- Open the **Time Periods** dialog, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the table toolbar. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Button Visibility

Button Visibility

The button "Time Period" is visible **only** in the **Forecast** diagram or any diagram containing a forecast.

##### Specifying Timelines

When specifying timelines, you can either a pre-existing timeline or a new one.

timelineTo specify the **Timeline**

There are two things you can do here:

- select Select a pre-existing Timeline.
  1. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='alt+down.png'] , or Alt+Down for a drop-down menu to make your selection. [ATTACHMENT filename='specify_timeline.png']
- newtimeline Create a new Timeline.
  1. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='three_dot_edit_small.png'] for a **Select Timeline** dialog.
  2. Click Creation Mode. [ATTACHMENT filename='turned_on_creation_mode.png']
  3. Select the appropriate package from the list in which you want to store a new Timeline.
  4. Click Create. (Shortcut: Right-click and click Create. ) The new Timeline's specification window opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='Create_timeline_button.png']
  5. Type the name of the new Timeline. Click Close. [ATTACHMENT filename='Specifying_name_of_new_timeline.png']
  6. Click OK.

1014964888

INLINE

The model used in the figures on this page is the **DoDAF Sample**. To open this sample, go to*<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\UPDM\DoDAF Sample.mdzip*

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738364 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04182: (2026x) Timer Boundary Event

- page_id: `272738364`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738364/2026x+Timer+Boundary+Event
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:45:00.417+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) BPMN modeling > (2026x) Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > (2026x) BPMN Process Diagram > (2026x) Boundary Events
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A Timer Boundary Event, which is attached to the boundary of an Activity, change a normal flow into an exception flow upon being triggered. A particular time-date or cycle, for example, on Mondays at 9 A.M., can be specified to trigger a Timer Boundary Event.

A Timer Boundary Event displays any of the following on a diagram:

- A Timer Boundary Event name if the name is specified.
- A Time Cycle property name if the name is unspecified.
- A Time Date property if the name and Time Cycle are unspecified.

**Notation**

- Timer Boundary Event (Cancel Activity - True) [ATTACHMENT filename='boundary_event_timer.png']
- Timer Boundary Event (Cancel Activity - False) [ATTACHMENT filename='boundary_event_timer_false.png']

**Related elements**

- Boundary Events
- Activities

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram
- BPMN Choreography Diagram

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating and Using a BPMN Event' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

A particular time-date or cycle, for example, on Mondays at 9 A.M., can be specified to trigger a Timer Boundary Event.</p><p>A Timer Boundary Event displays any of the following on a diagram:</p><ul><li data-uuid="662ae146-4040-4316-b6c3-04179e79adf1">A Timer Boundary Event name if the name is specified.</li><li data-uuid="932e09ef-571c-4551-a74e-a61b89a14670">A Time Cycle property name if the name is unspecified.</li><li data-uuid="c0876345-e4fa-48dd-a6bf-81af2dbd375a">A Time Date property if the name and Time Cycle are unspecified.</li></ul><p><strong>Notation</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="05570628-965f-4028-a548-694a95acda6a">Timer Boundary Event (Cancel Activity - True)<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="boundary_event_timer.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Timer Boundary Event" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li data-uuid="9271a25c-1507-4516-b33c-81c6eee96b56">Timer Boundary Event (Cancel Activity - False)<br /><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="55"><ri:attachment ri:filename="boundary_event_timer_false.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Timer Boundary Event" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Boundary+Events">Boundary Events</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Activities">Activities</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related diagrams</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Process+Diagram">BPMN Process Diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Collaboration+Diagram">BPMN Collaboration Diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Choreography+Diagram">BPMN Choreography Diagram</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedure</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating and Using a BPMN Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating and Using an Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

## PAGE 04183: (2026x) Timer Catching Intermediate Event

- page_id: `272738355`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738355/2026x+Timer+Catching+Intermediate+Event
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:44:44.597+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) BPMN modeling > (2026x) Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > (2026x) BPMN Process Diagram > (2026x) Intermediate Catch Event
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A Timer Catching Intermediate Event acts as a delay mechanism based on a particular time and date, or cycle, for example, on Mondays at 9 A.M.

This Event displays any of the following on a diagram:

- A Timer Catching Intermediate Event name if the name is specified.
- A Time Cycle property name if the name is unspecified.
- A Time Date property if the name and Time Cycle are unspecified.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related element**

- Intermediate Catch Event

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram
- BPMN Choreography Diagram

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating and Using a BPMN Event' space='']

## PAGE 04184: (2026x) Timer Start Event

- page_id: `272737859`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272737859/2026x+Timer+Start+Event
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:44:34.165+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) BPMN modeling > (2026x) Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > (2026x) BPMN Process Diagram > (2026x) Start Events
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A Timer Start Event allows a particular time and date or cycle setting, for example, on Mondays at 9 A.M., to trigger the start of a process.

A Timer Start Event displays any of the following on a diagram:

- A Timer Start Event name if the name is specified.
- A Time Cycle property name if the name is unspecified.
- A Time Date property if the name and Time Cycle are unspecified.

Notation

- Interrupting Timer Start Event [ATTACHMENT filename='start_event_timer_interr.png']
- Non-interrupting Timer Start Event [ATTACHMENT filename='start_event_timer_noninterr.png']

**Related element**

- Start Events

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram
- BPMN Choreography Diagram

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating and Using a BPMN Event' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738957 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04185: (2026x) Tool interface

- page_id: `272738957`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738957/2026x+Tool+interface
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:50:52.488+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Appendix A: report extensions
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Custom Tool is written in the Java language. The tool implements a specialized interface called ITool. The Report API provides both an interface and a class to support interface realization and class generalization. As mentioned earlier, a Tool is modeled on the JavaBean specifications. Functions implemented in this class must be defined in a series of setter or getter methods. The following sample shows the source code for the ITool interface that you must implement:

```text

```

All tools must implement the ITool interface (or one of its subclasses) as it defines the methods the template engine calls to execute. The table below provides a description of the methods in the ITool interface.

The table below lists Description of Methods in ITool interface.

| Method | Description |
| --- | --- |
| setContext(Context context) | Once the class has been initialized, this method is called by the template engine runtime to set the template context. Overriding the current context will affect the code after this tool. |
| getContext() | Return the template context that is assigned to the current runtime. |
| setProperties(Properties properties) | This method is called by the template engine runtime, once the class has been initialized, to set the template properties. Overriding the current properties will not affect the engine. |
| getProperties() | Return the current template properties. |
| VOID | Represents the Void class. VOID is used to make sure that returning data from the setter method is absolutely nothing. In general, Velocity considers the return of void from the setter method as a ‘null’ value. This causes the word 'null' to be printed out in the report. To avoid this problem, the setter method may use VOID as a return object. |
| class Void | A void class is used as a return in the Tool when you want to be sure that nothing is returned to the context. |
| class RetainedString | A direct command report formatter to keep the referenced String format. The formatter and other reference insertion handlers should maintain Strings directed by this class. |

The other classes that can be extended are Tool and ConcurrentTool.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### ITool Interface And Related Class.

#### Class Tool

A Class Tool is a base class realizing the interface ITool. This class provides the default implementation for ITool.

This class also provides methods derived from the java.util.Observable class, which can be used to notify the observers. An observer class, such as a template engine or a graphical user interface class, can receive the notification message from the tool and manage to display or interact with the user.

#### Concurrent Tool

A Concurrent Tool provides concurrent tasks running in the template engine. This class implements an unbounded thread-safe queue, which arranges the elements in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) order. New tasks are inserted at the tail of the queue, and the queue retrieval operations obtain elements at the head of the queue.

The tool extends from this class and is ideal for processing a long task that does not want other tasks to wait until the process is complete. The following code shows the sample usage of Concurrent Tool.

```text

```

An example of Concurrent Tool is *FileTool*. The root template that calls the **file tool** method will create a subprocess, offer the subprocess into a queue, and continue the root template until finished. The template engine will later call the **consume** method to complete the subprocess once the consumer queue is available. The number of concurrent processes available for the execution is declared in the template engine property (See **TemplateConstants.TEMPLATE_POOL_SIZE** for detail.).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Custom Tool is written in the Java language. The tool implements a specialized interface called ITool. The Report API provides both an interface and a class to support interface realization and class generalization. As mentioned earlier, a Tool is modeled on the JavaBean specifications. Functions implemented in this class must be defined in a series of setter or getter methods. The following sample shows the source code for the ITool interface that you must implement:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3f871750-90ad-43ef-8905-892179d56e4e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public interface ITool extends IBean
{
   // attributes
   Void VOID = new Void();
   // inner classes
   public class Void{}
   public class RetainedString implements CharSequence{}
   // methods 
   void setContext(IContext context);
   String getContext();
   void setProperties(Properties properties);
   Properties getProperties();
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>All tools must implement the ITool interface (or one of its subclasses) as it defines the methods the template engine calls to execute. The table below provides a description of the methods in the ITool interface.</p><p>The table below lists Description of Methods in ITool interface.</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Method</th><th> Description</th></tr><tr><td>setContext(Context context)</td><td>Once the class has been initialized, <br />this method is called by the template <br />engine runtime to set the template <br />context. Overriding the current context <br />will affect the code after this tool.</td></tr><tr><td>getContext()</td><td>Return the template context that is <br />assigned to the current runtime.</td></tr><tr><td>setProperties(Properties properties)</td><td>This method is called by the template <br />engine runtime, once the class has <br />been initialized, to set the template <br />properties. Overriding the current <br />properties will not affect the engine.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">getProperties()</td><td colspan="1">Return the current template properties.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">VOID</td><td colspan="1">Represents the Void class. VOID is used <br />to make sure that returning data from the <br />setter method is absolutely nothing. In <br />general, Velocity considers the return of void <br />from the setter method as a ‘null’ value. <br />This causes the word 'null' to be printed <br />out in the report. To avoid this problem, <br />the setter method may use VOID as a return <br />object.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">class Void</td><td colspan="1"><p>A void class is used as a return in the Tool <br />when you want to be sure that nothing is <br />returned to the context.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">class RetainedString</td><td colspan="1">A direct command report formatter <br />to keep the referenced String format. <br />The formatter and other reference insertion <br />handlers should maintain Strings directed by <br />this class.</td></tr></tbody></table><p>The other classes that can be extended are Tool and ConcurrentTool. </p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="ITool Interface And Related Class." ac:alt="ITool Interface And Related Class."><ri:attachment ri:filename="Class Diagram.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Tool interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">ITool Interface And Related Class.</h6><h2>Class Tool</h2><p>A Class Tool is a base class realizing the interface ITool. This class provides the default implementation for ITool.</p><p>This class also provides methods derived from the java.util.Observable class, which can be used to notify the observers. An observer class, such as a template engine or a graphical user interface class, can receive the notification message from the tool and manage to display or interact with the user.</p><h2>Concurrent Tool</h2><p>A Concurrent Tool provides concurrent tasks running in the template engine. This class implements an unbounded thread-safe queue, which arranges the elements in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) order. New tasks are inserted at the tail of the queue, and the queue retrieval operations obtain elements at the head of the queue.</p><p>The tool extends from this class and is ideal for processing a long task that does not want other tasks to wait until the process is complete. The following code shows the sample usage of Concurrent Tool.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9b569d5c-816c-43b7-af13-12a47a93233f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.nomagic.magicreport.engine.ConcurrentTool;
public class LongTaskTool extends ConcurrentTool
{
   public String longTask() {
      // enqueue object
      offer(new ConsumeObject(referent));
   }
   public void consume(ConsumeObject consumeObject) {
      Object referent = consumeObject.get();
      // process long task
   }
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>An example of Concurrent Tool is <em>FileTool</em>. The root template that calls the <strong>file tool</strong> method will create a subprocess, offer the subprocess into a queue, and continue the root template until finished. The template engine will later call the <strong>consume</strong> method to complete the subprocess once the consumer queue is available. The number of concurrent processes available for the execution is declared in the template engine property (See <strong>TemplateConstants.TEMPLATE_POOL_SIZE</strong> for detail.).</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272737898 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04186: (2026x) Toolbars

- page_id: `272737898`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272737898/2026x+Toolbars
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:49:22.280+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Getting started > (2026x) Understanding the user interface
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Restoring default toolbars

INLINE

Toolbars help to speed up your work when performing commonly used tasks. Your modeling tool has two types of toolbars:

- Main toolbars - contain commands for basic project-related tasks and are located at the top of the modeling tool window, right under the main menu.
- Diagram toolbars - contain commands for working with diagram symbols and are located at the top of the diagram pane.

To show or hide different toolbars

- In the main menu, click View , select either Main Toolbars or Diagram Toolbars , and in the displayed menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.
- Right-click the desired toolbar area and in the shortcut menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.

##### Customizing toolbars

You can customize existing toolbars by adding or removing buttons and also create new custom toolbars.

To add a new toolbar

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the desired toolbar group and click the Add > New Toolbar .
3. Enter the name of the new toolbar and click OK .

After completing the above steps, a new toolbar is created in the selected toolbar group, and you can choose to show/hide it in the shortcut menu of the respective toolbar area. Note that the newly created toolbar does not have any buttons, so you need to add them after creating it.

To add a new button to a toolbar

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the desired toolbar and click the Add > Button . [ATTACHMENT filename='customize_toolbars_dialog.png']
3. In the Add Button dialog, select the desired button or command and click OK .
4. Click OK one more time to close the Customize Toolbars dialog.

A new button is added to the selected toolbar. To add more than one button, repeat steps 2 and 3 as many times as you need.

To remove a toolbar or button

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the toolbar or button you want to remove and click the Remove button.

After completing the above steps, the selected toolbar or button is removed from the respective toolbar area or toolbar. Toolbars that have been removed do not appear in the menu for selecting which toolbars you want to show.

#### NOTE: Restoring default toolbars

Restoring default toolbars

To undo all the actions for customizing toolbars, open the **Customize Toolbars** dialog, as described in step 1 above, and click the **Reset to Defaults** button.

1421810869

INLINE

Note that the newly created toolbar does not have any buttons, so you need to add them after creating it.</p><p><br /></p><p>To add a new button to a toolbar</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the main menu, select <strong>View</strong> &gt; <strong>Main Toolbars</strong>/<strong>Diagram Toolbars</strong> &gt; <strong>Customize</strong>.</li><li>Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select <strong>Customize</strong>.</li></ul></li><li>In the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, select the desired toolbar and click the <strong>Add</strong> &gt;<strong> Button</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="customize_toolbars_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Toolbars" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> </li><li>In the <strong>Add Button</strong> dialog, select the desired button or command and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> one more time to close the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog.</li></ol><p><br />A new button is added to the selected toolbar. To add more than one button, repeat steps 2 and 3 as many times as you need.</p><p><br /></p><p>To remove a toolbar or button</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the main menu, select <strong>View</strong> &gt; <strong>Main Toolbars</strong>/<strong>Diagram Toolbars</strong> &gt; <strong>Customize</strong>.</li><li>Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select <strong>Customize</strong>.</li></ul></li><li>In the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, select the toolbar or button you want to remove and click the <strong>Remove</strong> button.</li></ol><p><br />After completing the above steps, the selected toolbar or button is removed from the respective toolbar area or toolbar. Toolbars that have been removed do not appear in the menu for selecting which toolbars you want to show.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="67428948-3ddd-4460-9978-eac77bf76b73"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Restoring default toolbars</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To undo all the actions for customizing toolbars, open the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, as described in step 1 above, and click the <strong>Reset to Defaults</strong> button.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="947a133f-8ff7-4609-a416-b63e2de23099"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1421810869</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Understanding the user interface" /></ac:link></li><li class="_mce_tagged_br"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Main menu" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Model Browser" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Image Library tab" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729613 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04188: (2026x) Top level elements

- page_id: `272729613`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729613/2026x+Top+level+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:35:24.131+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Data-related modeling > (2026x) Database support > (2026x) Database modeling
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There are several top-level model elements, that serve as the containers for other model elements of the database model: Database, Schema, and Catalog.

Top level elements are not strictly necessary to begin database modeling. You can start modeling database elements (like tables) in the standard UML package (even directly under root ‘Data’ model element). But top level elements help to provide context for those other elements and their naming and positioning in the database. So, at least one top level element should be present - either Schema element or Database element. Optimally both Database and Schema element should be present in the model (Schema package inside the Database package). Catalog modeling is less important, it can be skipped. Not all databases have support for catalogs.

When top-level element is created (either on the diagram or in the containment tree), a special dialog is shown for selecting database flavor.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Database flavor selection dialog.

When DB flavor is chosen, the necessary profile for that DB flavor is attached to the project (providing standard data types for that DBMS and / or additional stereotypes for modeling extensions of that DB flavor). Then profile application relationship is created from the package that is being created (Database, Schema) to the attached DB profile. This marks the top level element as belonging to this DB flavor, Other DB elements, created under that top level element will be automatically considered as belonging to this DB flavor.

If you would like to switch database flavor after creating a top level element, you can do this in the following way.

To switch database flavor after creating a top level element

#### WARNING: Important

Important

You must have the necessary module attached to your project (Use **File>Use Module**menu then choose the necessary module from your **<install.root>\profiles**predefined location.).

1. Right-click the top level element.
2. From the shortcut menu, select Apply Profiles .
3. Select the check box near the needed profile and clear the check box near the old profile.
4. Click Apply .

Top level elements can be explicitly drawn on the diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Database top level containers (database and schema) on the diagram pane.

However, showing top level elements on the diagram, and nesting their contents inside them is often clumsy, and consumes valuable diagram space. Showing them on the diagram pane is not necessary; it is enough to create them in the Containment tree (using the **New Element**command on the shortcut menu). Then, place your diagram inside the created containers, and the elements that you will be creating in your diagram, will go into the necessary container. See the following figure (logically equivalent to the previous one), showing a top level element just in the Containment tree and not displayed on the diagram pane.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Database top level containers (database and schema) in Containment tree, but not on diagram pane.

There is also one additional complication, steming from the limitations of UML. UML does not allow placing UML properties (which are used for SQL sequence modeling), or operations (which are used for SQL stored procedure & function modeling) directly into packages. Properties and operations can only occur in classes. A special model element was introduced to work around this limitation - GLOBALS element (based on UML class). This intermediate element can be placed directly inside the top level element (usually Schema, but can also be placed under Database) and then the necessary database elements - sequences, stored procedures can be placed inside it.

##### Database

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Database is modeled as UML Package with Database stereotype applied.

Database is a top level element, representing entire database within DBMS. Besides the standard SQL element properties, database has the following properties available in the Specification window.

| Property name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Vendor | Specifies the vendor and the version of the database software. These fields are used for information purposes only. They do not affect the generation or further modeling. |
| Version |  |

##### Schema

#### NOTE: Note

Note

SQL Schema is modeled as UML Package with Schema stereotype applied.

Schema element represents a collection of database elements - tables, indexes, stored procedures, etc. - grouped for particular purpose (such as data structures for some particular application).

##### Catalog

#### NOTE: Note

Note

SQL Catalog is modeled as UML Package with Catalog stereotype applied.

Catalog element represents intermediate grouping level between database and schema. Catalogs are also reused for Oracle DB modeling - to implement Oracle packages.

##### GLOBALS

#### NOTE: Note

Note

GLOBALS is modeled as UML Class with the «Globals» stereotype applied.

GLOBALS element is a special intermediate element to work around limitation of UML. UML does not allow placing UML properties (which are used for SQL sequence modeling), or UML operations (which are used for SQL stored procedure & function modeling) directly into packages. Properties and operations can only occur in classes.

To work around this limitation, GLOBALS element (based on UML class) was introduced. This intermediate element can be placed directly inside the top level element (usually Schema, but can also be placed under Database) and then the necessary database elements - sequences, stored procedures and functions can be placed inside it.

Name of GLOBALS model element is not important, but for the sake of tidiness it should be named “GLOBALS”. There should be at most one such element per the container (Schema, Database, Package). This model element does not carry any additional useful properties; it serves just as a carrier of inner elements - sequences and routines.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

You can start modeling database elements (like tables) in the standard UML package (even directly under root ‘Data’ model element). But top level elements help to provide context for those other elements and their naming and positioning in the database. UML does not allow placing UML properties (which are used for SQL sequence modeling), or operations (which are used for SQL stored procedure &amp; function modeling) directly into packages. Properties and operations can only occur in classes. A special model element was introduced to work around this limitation - GLOBALS element (based on UML class). <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729208 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04189: (2026x) TotalDigits

- page_id: `272729208`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729208/2026x+TotalDigits
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:34:24.516+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Data-related modeling > (2026x) XML schemas > (2026x) XML schema mapping to UML elements
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Maps to a UML Attribute with the stereotype XSDtotalDigits. The name and type of such an attribute do not make sense.

- value – to Attribute initial value.

#### PANEL: totalDigits XML representation summary

totalDigits XML representation summary

```text
<totalDigits
```

```text
fixed = boolean : false
```

```text
id = ID
```

```text
value = positiveInteger
```

```text
{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}>	Content: (annotation?)
```

```text
</totalDigits>
```

#### PANEL: totalDigits XML code sample

totalDigits XML code sample

```text
The following is the definition of a user-derived datatype which could be used to represent monetary amounts, such as in a financial management application which does not have figures of $1M or more and only allows whole cents. This definition would appear in a schema authored by an 'end-user' and shows how to define a datatype by specifying facet values which constrain the range of the base type in a manner specific to the base type. (different than specifying max/min values as before).
```

```text
<simpleType name='amount'>
```

```text
<restriction base='decimal'>
```

```text
<totalDigits value='8'/>
```

```text
<fractionDigits value='2' fixed='true'/>
```

```text
</restriction>
```

```text
</simpleType>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
